meta.title: Добре дошли в Mikser
meta.description: Бърз генератор на статични сайтове, управляван от плъгини. Изграждайте сайтове с документи, оформления, ресурси и PDF експорт.

entities.name: en/notes
entities.content:
## Hello from LiquidJS

This document renders through **mikser-io-render-liquid**:

- `{{ ... | markdown }}` runs the body through `render-markdown`.
- `{{ date | date: "%Y-%m-%d" }}` uses LiquidJS's built-in date filter.
- `{{ author | default: "Mikser" }}` falls back if `meta.author` is unset.

That's it — every runtime function (e.g. `markdown`, `href`) is available as a Liquid filter without per-helper wiring.

meta.title: Welcome to Mikser
meta.description: A fast, plugin-driven static site generator. Build sites with documents, layouts, assets, and PDF exports — all from a single config.

entities.name: en/report
entities.content:
## Executive Summary

This document demonstrates Mikser's PDF postprocessor. It is authored in Markdown with YAML front-matter, rendered to HTML by the `report.html-pdf.hbs` layout, and then automatically converted to a print-ready PDF by the `post-pdf` plugin using a headless Chromium browser.

The same source file produces two output artefacts — `report.html` (intermediate) and `report.pdf` (final). The intermediate HTML file is deleted after the PDF is written.

## Plugin Pipeline

The pipeline that produces this document involves five plugins:

| Step | Plugin | Responsibility |
|------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | `front-matter` | Parse YAML front-matter from `.md` files |
| 2 | `layouts` | Match document to layout, set render options |
| 3 | `render-hbs` | Render the Handlebars template to HTML |
| 4 | `post-pdf` | Convert the rendered HTML to PDF via Puppeteer |
| 5 | `layouts` (onComplete) | Write PDF to disk, delete intermediate HTML |

## How PDF Generation Works

### Layout naming convention

The layout filename `report.html-pdf.hbs` encodes two pieces of information:

- **`.hbs`** — the template engine (Handlebars)
- **`.html-pdf`** — the output format is HTML with the `pdf` postprocessor

The `getFormatInfo` function in the layouts plugin parses this into `{ format: 'html', postprocessor: 'pdf', template: 'hbs' }`.

### Auto-promotion

After a successful render, the `onBeforePostprocess` hook in `engine.js` reads all RENDER journal entries that have `options.postprocessor` set and queues corresponding POSTPROCESS entries. No manual wiring is needed.

### Browser lifecycle

The `post-pdf` plugin maintains a single shared Puppeteer browser instance:

```js
// setup — called once per postprocess phase
browser = await puppeteer.launch({ headless: true })

// teardown — deferred by 60 s to allow watch-mode reuse
teardownTimer = setTimeout(() => browser.close(), 60_000)
```

If a new build starts within the 60-second window, the timer is cancelled and the existing browser is reused, avoiding a cold launch on every save.

## Configuration Reference

Override defaults in `mikser.config.js` under the `post-pdf` key:

```js
export default {
  plugins: ['documents', 'layouts', 'render-hbs', 'post-pdf'],
  'post-pdf': {
    launch: { executablePath: '/usr/bin/chromium' },
    navigation: { waitUntil: 'domcontentloaded' },
    pdf: {
      format: 'A4',
      printBackground: true,
      margin: { top: '2cm', bottom: '2cm', left: '2.5cm', right: '2.5cm' }
    },
    teardownDelay: 30_000
  }
}
```

## Output Summary

| Artefact | Path | Notes |
|----------|------|-------|
| Intermediate HTML | `out/en/report.html` | Written by render phase; deleted after PDF is produced |
| Final PDF | `out/en/report.pdf` | Print-ready A4, background graphics enabled |

entities.name: en/posts/post-2
entities.content:
Clean code is not about style guides or linters — it is about writing code that the next person (often future you) can read without frustration. A few rules cover most of the ground.

1. Name things clearly

A well-chosen name eliminates the need for a comment. Compare:

// bad
const d = new Date(ts * 1000)

// good
const publishedAt = new Date(timestampSeconds * 1000)

If you find yourself adding a comment to explain a variable, rename it instead.

2. Keep functions small

A function should do one thing. A practical test: if describing what it does requires the word "and", split it. Functions that fit on a screen are easier to test, easier to move, and easier to delete.

3. Delete unused code

Dead code is a reader tax. Every commented-out block, every unused import, every feature flag that was never cleaned up — all of it costs future readers time. Version control has the history; you do not need to keep it in the source.

4. Make the happy path obvious

Handle edge cases early and return, so the main logic is not buried in nested conditions:

// harder to follow
function process(item) {
    if (item) {
        if (item.active) {
            // ... main logic deeply nested
        }
    }
}

// cleaner
function process(item) {
    if (!item) return
    if (!item.active) return
    // main logic at the top level
}

5. Consistency beats perfection

A codebase where every file follows the same conventions is easier to navigate than one where each file reflects a different author's preferences. Pick an approach and apply it everywhere — even if it is not the optimal one.

The best code is the code your teammates can maintain without asking you questions.

These principles apply beyond application code. If you are building a Mikser site, the same rules apply to your layouts and templates — see Building Layouts with Handlebars for concrete examples.

meta.title: Tips for Writing Clean Code
meta.description: Five rules that cover most cases — naming, function size, consistency, and deleting dead code.

entities.name: en/posts/post-1
entities.content:
Mikser is a static site generator built around a simple idea: your content lives in documents, your templates live in layouts, and a small config wires them together. This post walks through the basics to get you up and running.

Project structure

A typical Mikser project looks like this:

my-site/
├── documents/        # your content (Markdown, HTML, YAML, …)
├── layouts/          # Handlebars templates
├── resources/        # referenced assets (images, videos)
├── mikser.config.js  # plugin list & options
└── out/              # generated output

Run mikser and the engine imports all documents, matches them to layouts, renders the HTML, and writes the output folder.

Defining a layout

Create a file in layouts/ with a double extension. The first extension sets the output format, the second sets the template engine:

  • post.html.hbs — renders HTML with Handlebars
  • feed.xml.hbs — renders XML with Handlebars
  • report.html-pdf.hbs — renders HTML and then converts it to PDF

Inside the layout, the current document is exposed as document. Metadata from front-matter lives under document.meta and the body content is in document.content.

Connecting documents to layouts

Set a layout key in the document's front-matter:

---
layout: post
title: My First Post
date: 2026-01-10
---

Alternatively, configure auto-layout matching in mikser.config.js so documents are picked up by naming convention — no front-matter needed.

Running a build

npx mikser --working-folder ./my-site

Add --watch to rebuild on file changes during development. The incremental watcher only processes the documents that actually changed.

Tip: pass --clear on the first run to start from a clean output folder.

Once you are comfortable with the basics, the next post covers writing clean, maintainable code — habits that pay off as your project grows.

meta.title: Getting Started with Mikser
meta.description: A quick introduction to documents, layouts, assets, and PDF generation out of the box.

meta.title: Welcome to Mikser
meta.body:
Thanks for installing Mikser. This email was rendered from a Handlebars
template into MJML markup, then compiled to inbox-safe HTML by post-mjml.

entities.name: en/posts/post-3
entities.content:
Mikser layouts are Handlebars templates with a double extension. The first extension defines the output format (.html, .xml, …), the second names the template engine (.hbs). This post covers the most useful patterns.

Accessing document data

Inside every layout the current document is available as document:

<!-- front-matter fields -->
{{document.meta.title}}
{{document.meta.date}}

<!-- rendered body content -->
{{{markdown document.content}}}

Use triple braces {{{…}}} when you want to output raw HTML — otherwise Handlebars will escape it.

Conditional blocks

{{#if document.meta.author}}
  <span>By {{document.meta.author}}</span>
{{/if}}

Iterating over arrays

Front-matter arrays (like tags) can be looped with #each:

{{#each document.meta.tags}}
  <span class="tag">{{this}}</span>
{{/each}}

The href helper

The render-href plugin registers an href() Handlebars helper that resolves any entity from the sitemap by its href value. This is the right way to create links between pages — the helper returns the entity's actual output destination, so links stay correct even when clean URLs are enabled:

{{#with (href '/blog/getting-started')}}
  <a href="{{link}}">{{meta.title}}</a>
{{/with}}

To resolve a specific language variant, pass the lang as a second argument: {{href '/blog/getting-started' 'bg'}}.

Resource and asset helpers

The render-resource and render-asset plugins register Handlebars helpers that resolve remote media through the resource and asset plugins:

<!-- original image URL resolved through the resource library -->
<img src="../public/{{get 'link' (resource document.meta.image)}}">

<!-- processed WebP via an asset preset -->
<img src="../public/{{asset 'small-image' (get 'name' (resource document.meta.image)) 'webp'}}">

PDF layouts

Add -pdf to the format segment of the layout filename to enable PDF post-processing:

layouts/
└── report.html-pdf.hbs   # renders HTML, then Puppeteer converts to PDF

The post-pdf plugin launches a headless Chromium instance, navigates to the rendered HTML file, and prints it to PDF using the browser's built-in print engine — so you get full CSS, page breaks, and print media queries for free.

The Puppeteer browser instance is shared across all PDF jobs in a single run and kept alive for 60 seconds after the build completes, so rapid watch-mode rebuilds reuse the same instance without relaunching.

This series started with Getting Started with Mikser — go back there if you need to set up a project from scratch before working on layouts.

meta.title: Building Layouts with Handlebars
meta.description: How to build reusable layouts using Handlebars helpers for markdown, resources, assets, and relative links.
